content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 24,
1995
George H. Eiser, III
City Attorney
City of National City
Office of the City Attorney
1243 National City Boulevard
National City,
CA 91950
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95--081
Dear Mr. Eiser:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Councilmember R. Mitchel Beauchamp who also serves as a
member of the Community Development Commission of the City of
National City regarding his responsibilities under the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
Do the conflict—of--interest provisions of the Act prohibit
Councilmember Beauchamp from participating in a decision to expand
the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-95-O81
Page
2
CONCLUSION
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit the
councilmember from participating in the decision because it is
reasonably foreseeable that an amendment to the existing
redevelopment plan will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on his
financial interests.
FACTS
On April 11,
1967, the Redevelopment Agency of National City
was created and the city council declared itself to be the
Community Development Commission of National City (the “CDC”).
The
CDC is currently considering whether to add 317 acres to the
existing National City Downtown Redevelopment Project.
This
decision would also entail renaming the project area and setting
new procedures and time limits regarding financing the project.
Councilmember Beauchamp owns the following interests within
the boundaries of the existing redevelopment project area.
1.
Personal Residence:
The councilmember owns a fee
interest in the property which is valued at more than $100,000.
2.
Residential Rental Property:
The councilmember owns two
properties,
both of which are improved with a residence.
Both are
valued at more than $100,000.
In addition,
both are rented on a month—to—month basis to
tenants who have been sources of income to the councilmember in
excess of $250 in the past 12 months.
Both are subject to a first
deed of trust in excess of $10,000.
3.
Business Interests:
The councilmember is an employee and
the president of Pacific Southwest Biological Services, Inc.,
a
business that engages in biological consulting and re—vegetation.
In addition, the councilmember is the sole owner of the business,
his interest valued at greater than $100,000.
He has also
received a loan from the business of $10,000 and is paying 7.35
percent interest for the loan.
The councilmember is the owner and editor for Sweetwater
River Press,
a publishing company.
The councilmember owns more
than 10 percent of the business.
His interest is valued at
between $10,000 and $100,000.
4.
Other Sources of Income:
The councilmember plays the
organ for two churches situated in the exiting project area.
The
councilmember has received $2,400 from the National City First
Baptist Church and $1,200 annually from the First Congregational
Church of National City.


Our File No.
A—95—081
Page
3
National City has a population of approximately 55,000 and is
8.55 square miles in area.
The city’s land area is approximately
7.34 square miles,
or 4,697 acres.
You also provided the
following data:
(1)
there are 918 industrial buildings citywide
and 722 in the new project area;
(2)
there are 6,668 single-family
residences city wide and 3,435 in the project area;
(3) there are
8,348 Multi—family residences city—wide and 6,479 in the project
area;
(4) there are 43 churches citywide and 29 in the project
area;
and
(5)
there are 2,130 businesses with sales tax permits
citywide and 1,724 in the project area.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making,
or using their official position to
influence a governmental decision in which they know or have
reason to know they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the official or on a
member of the official’s immediate family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a)
— (d)
Councilmember Beauchamp owns the following interests within
the boundaries of the existing redevelopment project area.


Our File No.
A—95-081
Page
4
1.
Three property interests including his personal residence
and two properties,
both of which are improved with a residence.
Each of the property interests are valued at more than $100,000.
(Section 87103(b).)
2.
The councilmember has various interests in two business
entities in the project area, Pacific Southwest Biological
Services, Inc.,
and Sweetwater River Press.
3.
In addition to the two tenants on the councilmember’s
rental property, the councilmember also receives income from two
churches situated in the existing project area.
Thus,
Councilmember Beauchamp may not make, participate in
making, or use his official position to influence a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally,
on any of these interests.
Foreseeability and Materiality
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
In the Armento Advice Letter,
No. A-90-499,
we considered the
impact of redevelopment decisions on property and businesses
within the redevelopment area:
The purpose of any redevelopment plan is to
promote sound development and redevelopment of
blighted areas.
The anticipated result of
redevelopment is an increase in property values and
an improved business climate within the project
area,
which benefits the community as a whole.
The
very nature of redevelopment projects has led the
Commission to find that it is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be a financial effect
on real property values and business interests
located within or near project areas.
[Citations
omitted.]
Thus, decisions related to the size of the redevelopment
area,
including decisions to add property to the redevelopment
area, are presumed to foreseeably affect the councilmember’s
economic interests.


Our File No.
A-95-081
Page 5
Materiality
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
when the effect of a decision on an official’s economic interest
will be material.
If the decision will materially affect y of
the councilmember’s economic interests, the councilmember will
have a conflict of interest in the decision.
Whether a financial effect on an official’s real property is
material depends on whether the property is directly involved in
the decision or indirectly involved.
(Regulation 18702.)
An
official’s real property interest is directly involved in a
decision if:
The decision is to designate the survey area,
to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area,
or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions;
and real property in which the
official has an interest,
or any part of it is
located within the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the redevelopment area.
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (D).
(emphasis added.)
Thus, the decision to add property to the redevelopment area
that currently encompasses the councilmember’s real property is
deemed to have a material financial effect on the property and the
councilmember may not participate.
“Public Generally” Exception
However, Regulation 18703 provides an exception to the
conflict-of-interest provisions if the effect on the official’s
interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.
For the “public generally” exception to apply,
a
decision must affect the official’s interests in substantially the
same manner as it would affect a significant segment of the
public.
(Regulation 18703.)
On September 7,
1993, the Commission adopted specific
standards to determine when a governmental decision will fall
within the “public generally” exception.
New Regulation 18703
provides that the “public generally” exception applies where both
subdivisions
(1)
and
(2)
apply.
(1)
significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:


Our File No.
A-95—08l
Page
6
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents, or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners,
or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents, or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade, or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
***
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the official’s.
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision
(a) (1)
of this
regulation.
As discussed above, the redevelopment decisions will
necessarily have an impact on the value of property and businesses
in that area.
Councilmember Beauchamp owns three pieces of
residential property and two businesses in the redevelopment area.
In addition, at least four persons who have been sources of income
to the councilmember are also located in there development area.
It would appear that the cumulative effect on the councilmember
will be far greater than the effect on persons that own a single
parcel or a business in the redevelopment area.
You have provided
no facts to indicate that a significant segment of the population
of the city will be affected in the same manner.
Since the effect
on the councilmember will be unique in comparison to the effect on


Our File No. A-95-081
Page
7
other persons in the redevelopment area, the “public generally”
exception would not apply.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.2
Sincerely
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
ohn W.
Wallace
!ounsel,
Legal Division
2
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1O per page.

distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on his
